key_insight: Higher environmental hydrogen pressure sharply reduces fracture toughness in X65 pipeline steel welded joints, shifting failure from ductile microvoid coalescence to brittle cracking.
engineering_relevance: Pipeline weld integrity is highly sensitive to hydrogen exposure, so elevated hydrogen service conditions can markedly increase fracture risk in WM and HAZ regions.
method: CTOD fracture toughness testing on welded-joint regions
Electron microscopy for fracture surface and microstructure analysis
Comparison under gaseous hydrogen pressures of 1.0 MPa and 7.2 MPa
evidence_signal: The CTOD values dropped substantially at 7.2 MPa, with reductions of 79% in weld metal and 84% in the heat-affected zone relative to 1.0 MPa.
watch_item: Results are specific to the tested weld/joint condition and hydrogen pressures, so service-life predictions should not be generalized without broader loading and environment validation.
paper_title: Impact of Environmental Hydrogen Pressure on Fracture Toughness and Fracture Behavior of X65 Pipeline Steel Welded Joints
